overview: Software-Defined Networking (SDN) separates a device's traditional functions into three planes and centralizes the Control Plane in a controller, enabling programmatic, API-driven network management instead of box-by-box CLI configuration.
definitions: Data Plane: forwards packets (de-encapsulation, re-encapsulation, table lookups).
Control Plane: decides how to forward (routing protocols, ARP, STP, MAC learning) — moved to a central controller in SDN.
Management Plane: device access/control (SSH, SNMP, Syslog).
Southbound Interface (SBI): controller-to-device (OpenFlow, OpFlex, NETCONF, CLI, SNMP).
Northbound Interface (NBI): controller-to-application, typically REST APIs.
core_concepts: SDN's core idea is centralizing the Control Plane in a controller so devices become simpler, controller-driven forwarders.
Spine-Leaf (Clos) topology: every Leaf switch connects to every Spine switch; Leaf switches never connect directly to each other — the standard modern data-center design.
protocol_operation: Controller pushes forwarding/control decisions to devices via the Southbound Interface.
Applications query/program the controller via the Northbound Interface (typically REST).
packet_flow: Application -> Northbound API (REST) -> SDN Controller -> Southbound protocol (OpenFlow/OpFlex/NETCONF) -> managed device's Data Plane forwards packets accordingly.
best_practices: Use a Spine-Leaf topology for new data-center fabrics to guarantee predictable, non-blocking east-west bandwidth.
interview_questions: Explain the three network planes and how SDN redistributes them.
What's the difference between a Southbound and a Northbound interface?
Why do Leaf switches never connect directly to each other in a Spine-Leaf design?
enterprise_notes: Cisco SDA is the concrete Cisco implementation of these SDN concepts applied to campus networking.
